aliases: South Indian egg pancake with onion

english: South Indian egg pancake with onion

food_subgroup: Fermented rice and lentil pancakes with egg topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general uttapam entry (FOODON_03311069); no specific egg-and-onion variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific uttapam code. A0EKN (Pancake) is the closest defensible parent category for a thick fermented rice-lentil pancake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for uttapam or this specific preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium uttappam with egg and onion topping, approximately 150 g, as typically served at a South Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (plain) and egg composition — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, combined with USDA FoodData Central egg data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain uttapam values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), which reports per-100g values for idli/uttapam-type fermented rice-lentil preparations. Egg contribution (approximately one medium egg per uttapam, ~15 g egg per 100 g final product) estimated using USDA FoodData Central entry for whole cooked egg (FDC ID 173424). Onion contribution is minor and consistent with ICMR-NIN vegetable data. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the fermented batter base (~75%) and egg topping (~15%) with onion (~10%). This is an adapted estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented rice-lentil foods — analogous estimate based on idli and dosa GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Gopalpura et al. (2010) and Shobana et al. (2011) peer-reviewed GI studies on South Indian fermented foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for egg-onion uttapam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 69 is estimated from the closest analogous fermented rice-lentil preparations: idli (GI ~69–77, University of Sydney GI Database) and plain uttapam (GI ~65–72 per peer-reviewed South Indian food studies). The egg and onion toppings have negligible effect on the overall GI of the carbohydrate-dominant base. A midpoint estimate of 69 (medium GI) is used. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (69 × 26.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 27.4, rounded to 27.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~465 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the moderate sodium content (~465 mg per serving), which represents approximately 20% of the 2,300 mg daily limit; portion control and avoidance of additional salt or high-sodium chutneys is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~69) and moderate carbohydrate load (~40 g per 150 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Uttappam with egg and onion has a medium GI (~69) and a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with low-GI accompaniments such as sambar or tomato chutney, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol from egg (~72 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content is relatively low and the fermented lentil base provides plant protein and fibre; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the egg-derived cholesterol (~108 mg per 150 g serving) and limit frequency if dietary cholesterol is a clinical concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Uttappam with Egg and Onion is a thick, soft South Indian pancake made from a fermented batter of ground rice and urad dal (split black lentil), topped with a beaten egg and finely chopped onion before being cooked on a lightly oiled griddle. Unlike the thinner dosa, uttappam has a spongy, slightly tangy interior from the fermentation process, with a lightly crisp base and a savoury egg-enriched surface. It is a popular breakfast and tiffin item across South India and is widely served at South Indian restaurants in Singapore, including banana-leaf establishments. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate amount of complex carbohydrates from the rice-lentil base, plant protein from the lentils, and additional complete protein and micronutrients from the egg. The fermentation process improves digestibility and bioavailability of B vitamins. With a medium glycemic index of approximately 69, it is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires monitoring for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its carbohydrate load and sodium content. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals. It is typically served with coconut chutney and sambar.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for egg-onion uttapam was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain uttapam/idli data combined with USDA egg data using a weighted composite approach. GI is an analogous estimate from idli/dosa data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted nutrient composition and estimated GI.
notes: 1. Cuisine reclassified from 'Malaysian' (input) to 'South Indian' based on the dish's established culinary origin; the restaurant (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant) is a South Indian establishment. 2. Food group reclassified from 'Dairy and eggs' (input) to 'Grains and staples' as the primary component is the fermented rice-lentil pancake base. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional uttapam preparation. 7. Halal status is marked false as egg uttapam may be prepared in kitchens handling non-halal items; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. GI estimated at 69 (medium) based on analogous fermented rice-lentil foods from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement found for egg-onion uttapam variant.
